coredata.dc:title: Paper-based visualization of auramine O in food and drug samples with carbon dots-incorporated fluorescent microspheres as sensing element
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Wenhui
Yang, Zhen-quan
Xiao, Lixia
Han, Jie
Guan, Tianzhu
Gong, Xiaojuan
Hu, Qin
coredata.dc:description: A paper-based assay for visualization of auramine O (AO) was for the first time established by using CFMs as a ratiometric fluorescent probe (RFP). The CFMs were melamine formaldehyde microspheres (MFMs) incorporated with carbon dots (CDs), where the CDs species as sensing units and MFMs as a signal amplification carrier. The proposed RFP can quantitatively measure AO content from 0.0 to 10.0 μM and exhibited an ultralow limit of detection (LOD, 15.7 nM). In particular, obvious luminescence color change of CFMs from blue to green was perceived with naked-eyes and therefore, a solution-based and a paper-based visualization platform were respectively proposed for on-site visual detection of AO with LODs of 1.15 μM and 0.83 μM, separately. Finally, those fluorescence methods were adopted in sensitively quantitative measurement of AO within various food and drug samples, providing new prospects for analysts and technical support in food quality monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent microspheres
Carbon dots
Signal amplification
Visualization
Auramine O

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Procedure for AO monitoring based on CFMs-based RFP
Smartphone application for the detection of AO
Fluorescence detection of AO in real samples
Validation of method accuracy by HPLC
Detection of AO by CFMs-based RFP
Research on selectivity and anti-interference capacity of the CFMs-based RFP
Fluorescent test papers used to detect AO
Application of CFMs-based RFP for AO sensing in real food and drug samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The financial supports from Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province, China (BK20200949) and National Natural Science Foundation of China (21922202) are gratefully acknowledged.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The UV–Vis absorption spectrum (black line) of AO, the excitation spectrum (green line) and the emission spectrum (yellow line) of CFMs. (B) Changes in the CFMs fluorescence decay curve before (black line) and after (red line) the addition of AO. (C) The UV–Vis absorption intensity of CFMs with different volumes of AO added (from bottom to top): 0.0–50.0 μM. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) The SEM image of CFMs, and the inset is the histogram of the particle size distribution of CFMs. (B) The UV–Vis absorption spectrum (purple section), the excitation spectrum (green section) and the emission spectrum (yellow section) of CFMs, and the illustrations show CFMs under fluorescent light (left) and under UV light (right). (C) Fluorescent microscopic image of CFMs under laser excitation at 405 nm. (D) Fluorescence spectrums of CFMs at different excitation wavelengths (300 nm–400 nm). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) The fluorescence intensity of CFMs-based RFP varied with different contents of AO (from top to bottom): 0.0–50.0 μM. (B) CIE chromaticity diagram of CFMs-based RFP in the presence of different concentrations of AO (0.0–50.0 μM). (C) The plot of linearity of I0/I versus AO concentrations (0.0–10.0 μM).

(A) Smartphone recognition of CFMs-based RFP color changes in the AO concentration range of 0.0–120.0 μM. (B) Linear plot of CFMs-based RFP color change (green channel/blue channel) versus AO concentration. (C) The visualization of fluorescent test papers color of AO concentration in the range 0.0–120.0 μM. (D) Linear curves obtained by plotting G/B versus AO concentration. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Sketch map showing of CFMs preparation and its fluorescence quenching phenomenon caused by AO.
Results of AO detection in real samples by (A) CFMs-based RFP system and (B) Fluorescent paper-based sensing combined with smartphone applications (n = 5).

head.ce:title: Paper-based visualization of auramine O in food and drug samples with carbon dots-incorporated fluorescent microspheres as sensing element

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Engineering, Yangzhou University, Yangzhou, Jiangsu 225001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science and Engineering, Yangzhou University, Yangzhou, Jiangsu 225001, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yangzhou University, Yangzhou, Jiangsu 225001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yangzhou University, Yangzhou, Jiangsu 225001, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Environmental Science, and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Environmental Science, and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Environmental Science, and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A paper-based assay for visualization of auramine O (AO) was for the first time established by using CFMs as a ratiometric fluorescent probe (RFP). The CFMs were melamine formaldehyde microspheres (MFMs) incorporated with carbon dots (CDs), where the CDs species as sensing units and MFMs as a signal amplification carrier. The proposed RFP can quantitatively measure AO content from 0.0 to 10.0 μM and exhibited an ultralow limit of detection (LOD, 15.7 nM). In particular, obvious luminescence color change of CFMs from blue to green was perceived with naked-eyes and therefore, a solution-based and a paper-based visualization platform were respectively proposed for on-site visual detection of AO with LODs of 1.15 μM and 0.83 μM, separately. Finally, those fluorescence methods were adopted in sensitively quantitative measurement of AO within various food and drug samples, providing new prospects for analysts and technical support in food quality monitoring.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Organic colorants have invoked increasing scientific interest because of its striking implications in in pharmaceutical and food industries. Auramine O (AO, 4,4′-dimethylaminobenzophenonimide) is a banned detrimental dye due to horrible threat to human life (Yan et al., 2016). Apart from causing allergic and asthma reactions (Li et al., 2014), AO can also lead to DNA damage (Parodi et al., 1982) and can possibly causing cancer (Zhao et al., 2011; Zheng et al., 2012; Zou et al., 2013). Studies showed that long-term exposure to AO would horribly increase the risk of bladder and lymphatic cancer (de Jesus Azevedo et al., 2021; Parodi et al., 1982). The International Agency for Research on Cancer (IARC) has categorized AO into Group 2B carcinogenic compound (M. Ghaedi et al., 2015). The usage of AO in food and drug industries has been strictly and completely forbidden in many countries. For example, in United States, European Union, China, Japan, India, Philippines and Vietnam, no AO is allowed to be detected in foods and drugs (Dixit et al., 2011; Mehrorang Ghaedi et al., 2015; Nguyen Thi Kim et al., 2019; Tatebe et al., 2014; Yan et al., 2016). However, the problem of AO abuse in pharmaceutical and food industries still happened from time to time under the drive of commercial benefits (Abe et al., 2019; Van Dao et al., 2018). The use of AO in food industry has been even rising in some developing countries such as Vietnam (Nguyen Thi Kim et al., 2019). As such, developing an effective analytical method to monitor AO within food and drug samples is highly important.

Various analytical approaches have been designed to detect AO, including high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Tang et al., 2018; Tatebe et al., 2014), liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) (Li et al., 2014; Li, Ding, et al., 2013), voltammetry (Hoa et al., 2020; Nguyen Thi Kim et al., 2019), gas chromatography (GC) (Narvekar & Srivastava, 2002; Straub et al., 1993), microchip capillary electrophoresis (Li, Zhai, et al., 2013; Zhai et al., 2013), terahertz spectroscopy (Zhang et al., 2017) and Raman spectroscopy (Zhang et al., 2019). However, these technologies usually require tedious experimental steps, large and expensive instrumentation, time-consuming analysis, and/or highly skilled personnel, which largely limited their application scope especially in the case of portable on-site detection. Recently, fluorescence methods especially the carbon nanodots-based (CDs) nanoprobes opened up new prospects for food colorants detection and exhibited a bunch of benefits in rapidity, high sensitivity, good selectivity as well as remarkable visualization potential for possible on-site sensing platform construction (Han et al., 2016; Zhu et al., 2015).

Fluorescence CDs are oxygen-rich carbon nanoparticles that are quasi-spherical in shape with the mean size of ≤ 10 nm (Bourlinos et al., 2008). The CDs were reported to have appealing photoluminescence properties such as tunable emission and high photo-stability, the CDs have been widely used as fluorescence probe for sensing applications (Zhu et al., 2022). In the initial stage of using CDs for sensing work, CDs with a single emission peak was used as probe to construct a sensing system and the detection principle was based on the quenching effect on CDs fluorescence. However, the CDs probe with single signal output was easily susceptible to background or external interfering factors. Fortunately, the appearance of CDs-based ratiometric fluorescent probe (RFP) with multi-signal output wangled out of the difficulty due to its built-in correction ability (Han et al., 2020). More importantly, the ability of a RFP that can integrate with a smartphone or a test paper for visual on-site detection enabled the construction of a portable rapid analytical platform (Wang et al., 2020; Yang et al., 2021). So far, fluorescence paper assay based on CDs have been involved in food additives visualization. For example, Zhang et al. (Zhang et al., 2022) developed a paper-based ratiometric assay by using fluorescent CDs and gold nanoclusters as dual signal sources, which realized on-site rapid detection of glyphosate. Fan et al. (Fan et al., 2022) reported a portable paper sensor by using dual-emissive lanthanum-doped CDs (La-CDs) as a RFP for visual detection of tetracyclines in meat samples. Liu et al. (Liu et al., 2023) developed a paper analytical device by using gadolinium together with nitrogen co-doped CDs (Gd,N-CDs) with intrinsic dual emission as a RFP, which realized folic acid visualization in vegetable, yolk and fruit samples. Till the present, no report on the development of paper-based assay for visual detection of AO has been found.

Fluorescence signal amplification strategy has recently attracted tremendous attention in improving the sensitivity of a paper sensor. Building fluorescence microspheres with high surface probe density by doping or labeling fluorophores to the surface of microspheres (Zhou et al., 2021) has been a popular way to gain signal amplification and detection sensitivity enhancement. For example, Qin et al. (Qin et al., 2021) proposed signal amplification platform by loading N,S-CDs into melamine formaldehyde microspheres (MFMs) and realized ultrasensitive detection of Au3+ and its limit of detection (LOD) was 31.0 nM. Qiao et al. (Qiao et al., 2018) developed an effective fluorescence signal amplification scheme by doping CDs and polyethyleneimine (PEI) in silica nanoparticles through the reverse microemulsion route, which realized sensitivity enhancement for Cu2+ sensing with a LOD as low as 0.07 nM. Zhao et al. (Zhao et al., 2021) proposed a cell-based CDs microsphere with inactive S. aureus cells being a carrier and utilized it as an immunosensor to sensitively detect Escherichia coli O157. Of note, these CDs-based fluorescence microspheres (CFMs) had numerous advantages of good solubility, high sensitivity and well dispersion of microspheres.

In this work, an efficient paper-based assay was developed for visual detection of AO based on CFMs by using CFMs as sensing agent. The CFMs were prepared by loading CDs into MFMs (Scheme 1 ). Under an excitation of 380 nm, the CFMs exhibited fascinating dual-emission at 423 nm and 530 nm, respectively. After reaction with AO, the two fluorescence signals showed different fluorescence quenching degree. Therefore, a RFP based on CFMs was proposed. In addition, an obvious change in luminescence color from blue to green can be obviously captured by naked eyes under UV irradiation, based on which a solution-based sensing platform was constructed for AO detection. Furthermore, the proposed RFP was prepared as fluorescent test paper for the on-site AO detection. The feasibility of the proposed fluorescence methods for AO determination was confirmed and satisfactory results were obtained. Moreover, this is the first study on using CFMs as sensing agent and using the paper-based analytical device in AO sensing, opening novel prospects for food colorants monitoring in complex matrices.

AO was acquired in Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). O-phenylenediamine (OPD), melamine, formaldehyde (37.0–40.0%), hydrochloric acid (36.0–38.0%), citric acid and ascorbic acid were provided by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Fructose, glucose, diverse amino acids, all inorganic salts and anhydrous ethanol were obtained in Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Na3PO4, Na2HPO4, NaH2PO4, ammonium acetate and acetonitrile were bought in Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Brilliant blue G,amaranth, indigotin, quinoline yellow and sudan I were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Food and drug samples, including scutellaria powder, pollen typhae, corydalis tuber, curry power, bean curb and lemon candy, were obtained at local food markets in Yangzhou city, China. Ultra-pure water utilized in this work was produced with a Millipore-Q water purification system (Bedford, MA, USA). The reagents and chemicals were analytically pure and applied as received with no additional purification.

The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of CFMs and CDs were measured on a RF-5301PC fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Co., Ltd., Tokyo, Japan). The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectra of CFMs and CDs was carried out by a UV-2550 absorption spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Co., Ltd., Tokyo, Japan). The scanning electron microscope (SEM) images of CFMs were provided by a S-4800Ⅱ emission scanning electron microscopy (Hitachi Limited Co., Ltd., Tokyo, Japan). The morphology and size of CDs was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) using a CM100 transmission electron microscope (Philips, Eindhoven, Netherlands) with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) of CFMs and CDs were analyzed by a Cary 610/670 FTIR spectrometer (Varian, California, USA). The elemental analyses of CFMs and CDs were completed with an Elementar Analysen systeme vario EL cube organic elemental analyser (Hanau, Germany). The x-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement of CFMs and CDs were performed by an ESCALAB 250 x-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). The fluorescence decay curves of CFMs were recorded with a FLS-980 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh Co., Ltd., England). Fluorescence images of CFMs were observed by LSM 880NLO laser scanning confocal microscope (Carl Zeiss Jena, Germany).

The CFMs-based RFP was synthesized according to previous description after appropriate modification (Qin et al., 2021). Detailed steps for the synthesis of free-state CDs can be found in the supporting material. Firstly, 0.26 g of melamine and 2.49 mL of paraformaldehyde were dissolved into 4.51 mL of ultrapure water. Then, the mixture was subjected to stirring and heating for a 10 min period under 60 °C to obtain a clear and transparent MF pre-polymer solution. Then, MF pre-polymer solution (7.0 mL) was added into 10.0 mg/mL free-state CDs (7.6 mL) and diluted HCl (23.4 mL, 0.4 mM) and stirred for 30 min period under 100 ℃ to obtain a yellow MF-microsphere suspension. The suspension was subjected to 10 min centrifugation at 6,000 rpm. Meanwhile, the precipitate was gathered. To remove impurities, the precipitate was rinsed with abundant water and finally freeze-dried for long-term storage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for AO monitoring based on CFMs-based RFP
ce:section.ce:para: A CFMs-based RFP was established for detecting AO. In brief, 3.0 mg CFMs were dispersed into 10.0 mL ultrapure water to form a homogeneous CFMs solution (0.3 mg/mL). To assess linearity of the sensing system, 2.0 mL of CFMs solution (0.3 mg/mL) was added to a quartz cuvette. Then, AO at varying amounts was added (final concentrations 0.0–50.0 μM). The fluorescence intensities of the CFMs solution before (F0) and after (F) mixing with AO were acquired at 423 and 530 nm (λex = 380 nm), and the ratio values F0-423/F0-530 and F423/F530 were calculated and denoted as I0 and I, respectively. A linear calibration curve was constructed through plotting the ratio I0/I versus AO content. The LOD was calculated on the basis of 3δ/Ksv rule, in which δ represents the standard deviation of blank measurements that carried out by acquiring twelve consecutive scans of a blank CFMs sample. The Ksv is the slope of the linear curve.

The fluorescence response of the CFMs solution (0.3 mg/mL) to AO and the other possible interferences possibly co-existing with AO in real food and drug samples was observed to investigate selectivity of proposed RFP. The possible interfering substances investigated were diverse types of cations (Zn2+, Na+, Mg2+, Ag+, Ca2+, K+, Cu2+), anions (SO4 2-, Cl-, S2O3 2-, NO3 –, CO3 2–, Br-, I-), amino acids (glycine, l-methionine, glutamic acid, threonine, leucine, aspartic acid, phenylalanine, histidine, arginine), pigments (amaranth red, sudan Ⅰ, metanil yellow, quinoline yellow, indigo, orange Ⅱ, tartrazine) and the other possible small molecules (fructose, glucose and ascorbic acid). The fluorescence intensity change observations were conducted by adding substances to various tubes that contained 2.0 mL of CFMs solution (0.3 mg/mL) at the final concentration of 25.0 μM. I0/I were recorded at a λex of 380 nm.

In addition, the anti-interference capacity of the proposed RFP was also investigated through analyzing fluorescence response of the CFMs/AO mixture to those aforementioned interfering species. Firstly, a series of CFMs solutions (2.0 mL, 0.3 mg/mL) were transferred to tubes and then AO was introduced with an added content of 25.0 μM. Then, the above-mentioned interfering substances were added at a concentration of 25.0 μM, and the I0/I were recorded at a λex of 380 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone application for the detection of AO
ce:section.ce:para: The aqueous solutions of CFMs (0.3 mg/mL) containing different concentrations of AO (0.0–120.0 μM) were firstly prepared. Then, the color images of the CFMs/AO mixture were recorded in a dark room by a intelligent phone camera under irradiation with a 365 nm UV lamp, which was put in a fixed angle and position to reduce background interference. Color information (RGB values) of the captured images was explored using the color recognition function of a smartphone (ColorColl). The plot of the ratio of G value to B value (G/B) versus the concentration of AO was acquired to assess the sensing linearity. Five capture points on the images were selected for testing and the average value was calculated to provide the G/B data.

With aim of preparing fluorescent test papers, the cellulose filter paper (Whatman Co., Ltd., England) was cut into pieces of 1.0 × 3.0 cm2. Then, each piece of paper was immersed into an aqueous solution of CFMs (0.3 mg/mL) for 30 min. After that, the papers loaded with CFMs were dried naturally in air and used as fluorescent test paper for sensing work. The test papers incorporated with CFMs exhibited intensive blue emission under UV light at 365 nm.

The samples used in this work include scutellaria powder, pollen typhae, corydalis tuber, curry power, bean curb and lemon candy. All samples were firstly dried at 60 °C for 12 h under vacuum and crushed to a fine powder. Thereafter, each sample powder (0.1 g) were weighed, transferred into a 50.0 mL centrifuge tube before dissolution in a mixture of 10.0 mL of ultrapure water and anhydrous ethanol (v/v = 1:1), respectively. To assist through extraction, the mixture solutions were vigorously stirred for 1.0 min and then subjected to ultrasonic treatment for 30 min. Each mixture solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min. Then, the supernatants were harvested and filtered with the polystyrene microporous filter (pore size of 0.22 μM). In addition, filtrates were gathered and preserved under 4 °C in subsequent analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of AO in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, the suitability of the CFMs-based RFP for AO determination in the above food sample extractions was assessed. A series of 2.0 mL of CFMs solutions (0.3 mg/mL) were prepared and transferred into 5.0 mL centrifuge tubes. Subsequently, sample filtrates (20.0 μL) were added and blended through manual shaking, respectively. The fluorescence intensities of CFMs solution before and after mixing with each sample filtrate were measured at 423 and 530 nm (λex = 380 nm), and I0/I were calculated. All measurements were performed in five replicates. In terms of quantitative recovery test, AO at different concentration levels were spiked in each sample filtrate at the final concentrations 0.1, 0.2 and 0.3 μM, separately. Similarly, the total AO amounts were identified following those aforementioned steps for calculating quantitative recoveries.

Then, the feasibility of using a smartphone application for quantifying AO in the above food sample extractions was studied. In brief, each sample extract (20.0 µL) was supplemented to CFMs solution (2.0 mL, 0.3 mg/mL) and mixed completely. The fluorescence photo-images of the mixture were acquired and analyzed with the smartphone application (ColorColl) in order to get the corresponding RGB values under UV irradiation at 365 nm. The I0/I was calculated. For the quantitative recoveries tests, AO were spiked into each sample filtrate at three concentration levels of 10.0, 15.0 and 20.0 μM, respectively.

Furthermore, the suitability of paper-based assay for AO quantification in the above food sample extractions was investigated. Briefly, each sample extract (20.0 µL) was blended with 2.0 mL of CFMs solutions (0.3 mg/mL), separately. Then, papers were then immersed into each mixture solution for 30 min and dried naturally in air, respectively. The RGB value of each fluorescent paper was acquired by using a smartphone color recognition software (ColorColl) under a UV lamp at 365 nm, and the I0/I was calculated. For the quantitative recoveries tests, AO were spiked into each sample filtrate at three concentration levels of 5.0, 10.0 and 15.0 μM, respectively. Each data was averaged from five independent experiments and the standard deviations were calculated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Validation of method accuracy by HPLC
ce:section.ce:para: The HPLC analysis was conducted with an Agilent 1200 series HPLC using a photodiode array detector. A C18 column (150 × 4.6 mm) was used for separating the above-mentioned food sample extracts. The mobile phase included acetonitrile along with 10.0 mM ammonium acetate (v/v = 80:20), with flow rate, injection volume as well as column temperature being 1.0 mL min−1, 10.0 μL and 35 °C, separately. With the UV detection wavelength setting at 431 nm, the chromatograms were acquired.

The reason behind fluorescence intensity changes of CFMs caused by AO was firstly clarified to explore the detail sensing mechanism. As displayed in Fig. 1 A, the UV–vis absorption spectrum (black line) of AO accurately overlapped with the emission spectrum (yellow line) and partly overlapped with the excitation spectrum (green line) of CFMs, which suggests that the quenching mechanism was generated by inner filter effect (IFE) or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) (Xin et al., 2018). To clarify if the quenching phenomenon was resulted from a dynamic quenching or a static quenching process, the two exponential decay curves of CFMs with/without AO were obtained (Fig. 1B). By referring to the fitting results given in Table S1, the lifetimes of CFMs were 0.87 ns and 1.85 ns with an average lifetime of 2.72 ns. Following blending with AO, the lifetimes of CFMs were changes to 0.61 ns and 1.73 ns with an average lifetime of 2.34 ns. The apparent shortening of the fluorescence lifetime suggested that the existence of dynamic interaction between CFMs and AO. Then, the attempts were given to see if the fluorescence lifetime change of CFMs was resulted from the generation of new compound. Fig. 1C illustrated the UV–vis absorption spectrum of CFMs after mixing with various contents of AO. When CFMs was mixed with AO, the characteristic absorption band of AO at 431 nm was observed. No new peak was formed and no shift of the CFMs/AO mixture absorption band was observed as the added AO concentration increased, which implied that the interaction between CFMs and AO did not form new compound. Furthermore, zeta potential (ζ) measurement was carried out to investigate the interaction between CFMs and AO. As shown in Table S2, the ζ values of CFMs and AO were 29.77 and –1.11 mV, respectively. After reacting with AO, the ζ value of CFMs was decreased to 24.97 mV. The obvious charge states change of CFMs induced by AO clearly indicated the successful adsorption of AO on CFMs surface via electrostatic interactions. As such, it was deduced that the charge states change of CFMs caused by AO was the most likely cause of the fluorescence intensity change of CFMs. In addition, the electrostatic attraction between CFMs and AO means that their interaction meets the requirement of a FRET process where the distance between a FRET donor and an acceptor is less than 10 nm (Ma et al., 2022). As such, the quenching effect of AO on CFMs fluorescence was the result of the synergistic effect of IFE and FRET.
Accordingly, AO-induced of CFMs fluorescence quenching was referred to the pooled effects resulting from IFE, FRET, dynamic quenching and electrostatic interaction process.

The CFMs particle size and morphologies were studied through adopting SEM. As shown in Fig. 2 A, CFMs were in regular spherical structures with good dispersion. The particle size information of the CFMs was assessed by a Nano Measurer software v1.2. The histogram of particle size distribution was inserted in Fig. 2A. The CFMs was estimated to be dispersed in a size range of 2.93–5.16 μm, having an average particle size of 3.98 ± 0.5 μm. A partial enlarged view of Fig. S1A clearly showed with porous structure of the as-prepared CFMs. Meanwhile, the TEM image of free-state CDs was obtained and shown in Fig. S1B. The CDs had quasi-spherical structure and were uniformly dispersed in a range of 2.4–4.4 nm with an average diameter of 3.4 ± 0.3 nm.

Chemical composition of the as-prepared CFMs was firstly studied through elemental analysis. As shown in Table S3, the CFMs was consisted of N 41.22%, C 35.76%, H 4.40% and O 18.62%. It can be seen that the N content in CFMs was nearly six times higher than that in free-state CDs (7.16%, Table S3), suggesting the success formation of CFMs giving the fact that MFMs contain plenty of N element (Qin et al., 2019). Furthermore, XPS and FTIR were carried out to characterize the surface functionalities of CFMs. As shown in Fig. S2A, the full scan XPS spectrum of CFMs showed three main predominant peaks at 287.1, 398.1 and 531.1 eV, which were ascribed to C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively. The C1s spectrum (Fig. S2B) can be resolved into three distinct doublets of the sp2 carbon at 284.2 (CC), the sp3 carbon at 286.4 eV (CO) and the carboxyl carbon at 287.9 eV (–COOH), respectively. The N1s spectrum (Fig. S2C) showed characteristic peaks at 398.4, 399.3 and 402.0 eV, corresponding to the presence of CN, NH and NO, respectively. The XPS of O1s (Fig. S2D) can be decomposed into two characteristic peaks of CO (531.7 eV) and CO (533.1 eV). As displayed by the FTIR spectrum of CFMs (Fig. S4A), the absorption band at 3500–3200 cm−1 could be related to O H and N H stretching vibrations. The absorption peak at 2955 cm−1 was associated with C H stretching vibration. The strong absorption peaks at 1635–1454 cm−1 could be correlated with CO stretching vibration. The absorption peak at 1417–1253 cm−1 could be attributed to the CC bond motion. The tensile vibration of C N induced a weak peak at 1162 cm−1. The peaks at 1008 cm−1 were resulted from C O tensile vibrations. The results were consistent with XPS spectroscopic analysis. Meanwhile, the XPS (Fig. S3) and FTIR (Fig. S4B) spectra for free-state CDs we're obtained for comparison. The presence of characteristic functional groups of C N, N H, O H, CO, –COOH and C O clearly indicated that CDs were effectively anchored to the MFMs. The presence of hydrophilic groups including O H and N H groups on CFMs surface afforded its excellent water solubility, which is conducive to the subsequent sensing work.

The spectral properties of CFMs were assessed by acquiring its UV–vis absorption spectrum, fluorescence excitation spectrum and fluorescence emission spectrum. Fig. 2B showed that CFMs had two absorption peaks (purple section) at 213 and 275 nm. The stronger absorption peak at 213 was ascribed to π–π* transition of SP2 hybrid carbon (Ali et al., 2020) and the weak absorption peak at 275 nm was corresponding to n–π* electronic transitions of the CO bond (Hasan et al., 2018). The fluorescence emission spectrum of CFMs (yellow section, Fig. 2B) showed that the CFMs exhibited dual emission feature with two emission bands at 423 nm and 530 nm under a 380 nm excitation wavelength. Compared with the single emission feature of the free-state CDs (Fig. S5), the remarkable dual-emissive characteristic offered a possible opportunity for a RFP sensing system construction. The presence of multiple emission peaks was because of size effect and various functional groups on CFMs surface (Xin et al., 2018). The fluorescence excitation spectrum of CFMs (green section, Fig. 2B) demonstrated that the CFMs exhibited an excitation peak at 380 nm. As displayed by the inset in Fig. 2B, a CFMs aqueous solution appeared to be light yellow under day light and emitted blue fluorescence under irradiation at 365 nm. The fluorescent microscopic image of CFMs (Fig. 2C) and MFMs (Fig. S6) dried on a glass slide were also obtained using laser excitation at 405 nm. Simultaneous fluorescence emission from CFMs was clearly visualized under 405 nm laser excitation, which further confirmed that the CDs species were successfully anchored to the non-fluorescent MFMs. The emission spectra of CFMs at various excitation wavelengths were also acquired. Based on Fig. 2D, upon excited at 380 nm, the CFMs showed two emission peaks at 423 and 530 nm, separately, and the former had increased intensity compared with the latter. After increasing excitation wavelength from 300 to 400 nm, the position of the former band did not obviously change and its intensity (423 nm) reached maximum value when excitation wavelength was 380 nm; however, the later peak obviously red-shifted, and its intensity elevated as excitation wavelength increased. Of note, both the two emission bands (423 nm and 530 nm) had satisfactory emission intensity upon exciting by 380 nm and therefore, 380 nm was selected as optimal excitation wavelength for the subsequent sensing work.

To achieve optimal sensing performance, attempts were firstly dedicated to optimize the significant working parameters including CFMs concentration, pH and reaction time. Fig. S7A (red line) showed the effect of CFMs concentration on I0/I. The I0/I gradually increased as the CFMs concentration elevated from 0.0 to 0.3 mg/mL and further increasing of the CFMs concentration gave rise to a decreasing trend of I0/I. Therefore, 0.3 mg/mL of CFMs was chosen to be optimum concentration in later experiments. Subsequently, the fluctuation of I0 and I0/I with pH were investigated with the pH varied in a range of 2.0–12.0 (Fig. S7B, black line). Specifically, the I0/I gradually tended to increase when the pH increased from 2.0 to 5.0 and maintained almost constant within the pH range of 5.0–8.0, which afterwards witnessed a decreasing tendency at high pH values (≥9.0). The fluctuation of I0 and I0/I with pH was believed to be due to the dependency of charge state-related florescence intensity of CFMs and CFMs/AO on pH, which was commonly observed in a CDs-based sensing platform (Hu et al., 2021; Hu et al., 2020). To simplify experimental steps, CFMs was dissolved in ultrapure water (pH 7.0) for subsequent experiments. Furthermore, the impact of reaction time on I0/I was evaluated (Fig. S7C, blue line). The I0/I increased rapidly after AO and CFMs reacted for 1.0 min and it did not show obvious change through prolonging reaction time. It means that a short period of 1.0 min was enough for reaction completion, which allowed rapid detection of AO by the proposed sensing platform. Therefore, we chose 1.0 min to be optimum reaction time in later analyses.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of AO by CFMs-based RFP
ce:section.ce:para: The CFMs is expected to be used as a fluorescent probe for AO detection as it inherited the special affinity of CDs for AO. Under optimal experimental conditions, different concentrations of AO (0.0–50.0 μM) were introduced into a CFMs solution (0.3 mg/mL) and the fluorescence intensity at 423 nm (Fig. 3 A) were measured to assess the sensitivity of the constructed sensor. Based on Fig. 3A, the addition of AO resulted in different quenching extent on CFMs fluorescence intensity; the fluorescence intensity of CFMs at 423 nm notably decreased with increasing AO concentration (0.0–50.0 μM) while its fluorescence intensity at 530 nm was slightly affected. The visualization of color change of the CFMs solution induced by AO was realized by plotting CIE chromaticity diagram. As shown in Fig. 3B, gradual coordinates shift from blue to green under UV light was observed in the CFMs solution with the AO content increasing from 0.0 to 50.0 μM, which clearly indicated the color tonality changes of CFM after mixing with AO and offered great potential for the subsequent AO visualization application.

The plot of I0/I versus AO content showed a good linearity when the concentration of AO was within the 0.0–10.0 μM range (Fig. 3C). A fitted equation I0/I = 0.0934[C] + 0.9778 was achieved by the least-squares fitting method, where [C] referred to AO concentration. The LOD calculated in line with 3δ rule was 15.7 nM. The free-state CDs was used as a probe to sense AO and the detection performance was calculated for comparison (Fig. S8). A linear range was shown to be 0.0–10.0 μM with a LOD being 41.2 nM. Obviously, the CFMs-based RFP provided 3 times higher sensitivity than free-state CDs. Additionally, a comparison of the analytical performance of CFMs-based RFP to that of the other methods was displayed in Table S4. The sensitivity of the proposed RFP significantly increased compared with any of the existing methods including HPLC, voltammetry, capillary electrophoresis and the other fluorescence methods. Specifically, it was about 4.5 times higher than that of the previously constructed CDs-based probe, which clearly revealed the high sensitivity feature of the proposed CFMs-based RFP and loading CDs into a porous structure carrier indeed provide signal amplification function for sensitivity enhancement.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Research on selectivity and anti-interference capacity of the CFMs-based RFP
ce:section.ce:para: Validation of selectivity and anti-interference ability were highly necessary for a newly developed sensing platform. The selectivity of CFMs towards AO was investigated through observing the fluorescence response of CFMs towards AO and the other possible interfering species (various cations, anions, amino acids, pigments and some other small molecules that commonly exist in food samples) at 25.0 μM. Fig. S9 showed that only the introduction of AO induced significant fluorescence signal change of CFMs while the other possible interferences resulted in unobvious change of the fluorescence signal, implying that the CFMs-based RFP was only responsive to AO.

The anti-interference of CFMs towards AO was also studied. Firstly, a CFMs solution (0.3 mg/mL) containing 25.0 μM AO was prepared. Then, each of the above-mentioned interfering species was supplemented to mix at a concentration of 25.0 μM, respectively. Fig. S9 showed that the addition of possible interferences did not result in obvious change of the fluorescence signal of CFMs/AO, suggesting the excellent anti-interfering feature of CFMs towards AO sensing.

As shown in Fig. 4 A, the CFMs aqueous solution (0.3 mg/mL) showed a continuous color change from blue to green under UV irradiation at 365 nm after mixing with different amounts of AO (0.0–120.0 μM). The corresponding photo-images of CFMs/AO mixture solutions were taken by using a digital camera. The luminescence color information was converted to RGB values via the color recognition function of a smartphone. During photo-shooting, the green channel/blue channel ratio (G/B) was calculated to minimize external interference. The G/B value as well as AO concentration exhibited the favorable linear relation (R2 = 0.9952) within a 0.0–120.0 μM range (Fig. 4B) with a LOD of 1.15 μM was obtained, which allowed on-site detection of AO with a portable smartphone.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent test papers used to detect AO
ce:section.ce:para: In order to further promote the CFMs-based RFP for practical portable detection of AO, the fluorescence test papers were prepared. As provided in Fig. 4C, the luminescence color of the test paper exhibited blue emission under UV irradiation. When different amounts of AO solutions were dropped onto the test papers, striking luminescence color change was found; with increasing of AO concentration, the luminescence color of the test papers gradually changed from blue to green. The corresponding photo-images of the test papers were taken by a digital camera and the RGB values were obtained by the color recognition function of a smartphone. The linear correlation of AO concentration with G/B values was found over a 0.0–120.0 μM range (Fig. 4D), R2 = 0.9899, with the LOD being calculated to be 0.83 μM. The results showed the successful integrating of a smartphone application with fluorescence test paper for portable detection of AO.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of CFMs-based RFP for AO sensing in real food and drug samples
ce:section.ce:para: To validate whether the proposed CFMs-based fluorescence methods could be used to detect AO in real applications, various food and drug samples including scutellaria powder, corydalis tuber, pollen typhae, bean curb and lemon candy were selected as real samples. The assays were performed by using a fluorescence spectrometry and by a smartphone application (ColorColl). The detection results were listed in Table S5 and Table 1 A, respectively. The two fluorescence methods achieved consistent analyses results and satisfactory quantitative recoveries of 95.8%–112.2% and 98.4%–110.2%, respectively. The RSDs were below 2.47% (n = 5) and 4.65%, respectively, revealing the accuracy of the two methods. Meanwhile, our fluorescent test paper was used for detecting AO concentrations in these aforementioned food and drug samples. By color recognition function of a smartphone, the G/B values of the test paper color were obtained (Table 1B). Again, a good agreement among the results from the above three fluorescence methods was observed. The quantitative recoveries were 97.9%–118.5% and RSD ≤ 4.52% (n = 5). Furthermore, HPLC analysis of AO contents in the above samples was performed to verify the as-constructed fluorescence methods. Fig. S10 showed the chromatograms and linear curves. The analysis results in Table S5 were in good agreement with that acquired by the proposed fluorescence methods, unambiguously verifying their high accuracy feature. In essence, the proposed fluorescence methods were feasible and reliable for visual detection of AO in real food and drug samples.

In summary, this work presented a paper-based assay for AO visualization by using CFMs as a RFP. The CFMs were prepared by loading CDs into CFMs, where the CDs species served as fluorophores and MFMs acted as an amplification carrier. The proposed CFMs probe exhibited ultrahigh sensitivity and good selectivity towards AO sensing with a linear concentration range of 0.0–10.0 μM and a LOD of 15.7 nM were achieved. The fluorescence sensing mechanism was based on the different quenching degree on CFMs emission peaks at 423 and 530 nm via IFE, FRET, dynamic quenching and electrostatic interaction. Based on the color change of CFMs from blue to green after mixing with AO, a solution-based visualization platform and a paper-based device were prepared, obtaining LODs of 1.15 μM and 0.83 μM, separately. Additionally, the usefulness of the proposed fluorescence methods for real application was validated in food and drug samples. The results revealed that incorporating CFMs with signal amplification function was a useful strategy for constructing a highly sensitive RFP sensing system for AO detection, which can be expanded to design more sensing platforms for the other food sensing applications.

Wenhui Wang: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Zhen-quan Yang: Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Lixia Xiao: Formal analysis, Investigation. Jie Han: Methodology, Resources, Data curation. Tianzhu Guan: Investigation. Xiaojuan Gong: Formal analysis. Qin Hu: Investigation, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: Qin Hu reports financial support was provided by Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province, China. Jie Han reports financial support was provided by National Natural Science Foundation of China.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial supports from Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province, China (BK20200949) and National Natural Science Foundation of China (21922202) are gratefully acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2023.136890.

ce:section.ce:para:
